summary: Jeff Atwood argues that all web traffic should be encrypted by default, having evolved his position after the Snowden revelations and the 2016 US election. He presents three key reasons: privacy is an inalienable right that requires HTTPS, the performance penalty is now gone, and encryption prevents content tampering by ISPs and other intermediaries. The post highlights Let's Encrypt as a game-changing non-profit that provides free SSL certificates, breaking the cost barrier that previously prevented widespread adoption. Atwood concludes with a call to action for individuals and companies to donate to Let's Encrypt to support universal web encryption.
key_points: Without HTTPS, users have zero online privacy from WiFi snoopers, network providers, and governments
HTTPS now performs equal to or better than HTTP on modern devices, eliminating the historical performance excuse
ISPs like Comcast are actively injecting content into unencrypted web traffic, making tampering a real threat
Let's Encrypt is the first reliable free source of SSL certificates, removing the $30+/year cost barrier
Browser vendors and Google search rankings are actively pushing sites toward HTTPS adoption
money_quotes: Post Snowden, and particularly after the result of the last election here in the US, it's clear that everything on the web should be encrypted by default.
You have an inalienable right to privacy, both in the real world and online. And without HTTPS you have zero online privacy.
The performance penalty of HTTPS is gone, in fact, HTTPS arguably performs better than HTTP on modern devices.
Using HTTPS means nobody can tamper with the content in your web browser.
Imagine what it looks like when someone, or some large company, decides the rules don't apply to them?
Without that required cash each year, without the SSL certificate that you must re-purchase every year in perpetuity – you can't encrypt anything.
If you believe in an unalienable right to privacy on the Internet for every citizen in every nation, please support Let's Encrypt.
key_insight: Universal web encryption has become both technically feasible and morally imperative, and Let's Encrypt has removed the last major barrier by making SSL certificates free.
